sources.notes: Interactive problem: find two working batteries with a bounded number of pair queries.
Official proof places batteries on a cycle and uses the pigeonhole principle to bound the distance between two working ones.

annotation.summary: Find a pair of working batteries through interactive queries under a guarantee on the number of working batteries.
annotation.constraints: The query budget is bounded by floor(n^2/a), where a is the number of working batteries.
annotation.core_idea: Place the n batteries around a cycle. If a batteries work, the a circular gaps between consecutive working batteries sum to n, so one gap has length at most floor(n/a). Query every pair of positions at circular distance 1, then 2, and so on up to floor(n/a); one of these pairs must be two consecutive working batteries.

solution_variants.name: cyclic-distance-query
solution_variants.summary: For each distance up to floor(n/a), query all pairs at that cyclic distance until a working pair is found.

tags.evidence: The problem is solved by adaptively outputting pair queries and stopping when the judge confirms a working pair.
The query guarantee follows from a pigeonhole argument over a circular partition into gaps between working batteries.
The algorithm brute-forces every pair at all small cyclic distances up to the proven bound.
